coredata.dc:title: One step synthesis-functionalization of Carbon Quantum Dost by carbonization of beetroot fruit (Beta Vulgaris) bagasse and their performance in sensing of Ag+ and Cu2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Rojas-Valencia, O.G.
Díaz-Santiago, D.L.
Casas-Espínola, J.L.
Reza-San Germán, C.M.
Estrada Flores, M.
coredata.dc:description: Water-dispersible and fluorescence-stable carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been synthesized from waste Beetroot fruit (bagasse) through a low-cost synthesis-functionalization process at different times and temperatures of carbonization. At 254 nm of excitation, the obtained CQDs have emission in different colors: light purple, yellow and blue. By FT-IR analysis was determined that carbonyl groups from betalainic compounds present in beetroot fruit were responsible of surface passivation and consequently the presence of photoluminescent properties. Raman spectroscopy showed that the obtained CQDs are mainly made up of sp2 graphitic carbon with sp3 carbon defects and oxygen functional groups. The obtained CQDs were used in model Cu2+ and Ag+ solutions with the aim to study relationship of time and temperature carbonization with decrease of intensity emission to stablish their potential use as a sensor of metallic ions. The novelty of this work lies in obtaining CQDs by organic waste, which offers a solution from biomass waste disposal problems, especially in developing countries where these wastes are increasing considerably, and in addition the obtained product can be used as a metal ions sensor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
Bagasse
Beetroot fruit
Carbonization
Metal ions senser

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CQDs applied in sensing of CU2+ and Ag1+ ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The CQDs obtained at 100 °C, 200 °C, 300 °C and 400 °C (A, B, C and D) and 1, 2, 3 and 4 h of carbonization show luminescent emission properties.
Functional groups responsible from surface passivation of CQDs.
Dependence of time and temperature with intensity emission of CQDs for A4, B1, C4 and D2 samples.
Gaussian deconvulation of A1, B2, C3 and 4D samples.
Raman spectra of A1, B2, C3, D4 samples.
HRTEM images of CQDs obtained at 2 h and 400 °C of carbonization conditions.
Color emission of CQDs after being mixed with Cu2+ ions.
PL spectra of CQDs, A): 100 °C, B): 200 °C and C): 300 °C of carbonization temperatures before and after being mixed with CU2+ ions.
PL spectra of CQDs, A): 100 °C, B): 200 °C and C): 300 °C of carbonization temperatures before and after being mixed with CU2+ ions.
Change in optical properties of CQDs.

head.ce:title: One step synthesis-functionalization of Carbon Quantum Dost by carbonization of beetroot fruit (Beta Vulgaris) bagasse and their performance in sensing of Ag+ and Cu2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto Politécnico Nacional, ESIQIE, UPALM, Edificio Z5 segundo piso, Alcaldía Gustavo A. Madero, Ciudad de México, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instituto Politécnico Nacional, ESIQIE, UPALM, Edificio Z5 segundo piso, Alcaldía Gustavo A. Madero, Ciudad de México, Mexico

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Universidad Tecnológica de México – UNITEC México – Campus Atizapán

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto Politécnico Nacional, ESFM, UPALM, Laboratorio de Optoelectrónica, Alcaldía Gustavo A. Madero, Ciudad de México, Mexico
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Instituto Politécnico Nacional, ESFM, UPALM, Laboratorio de Optoelectrónica, Alcaldía Gustavo A. Madero, Ciudad de México, Mexico

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-dispersible and fluorescence-stable carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been synthesized from waste Beetroot fruit (bagasse) through a low-cost synthesis-functionalization process at different times and temperatures of carbonization. At 254 nm of excitation, the obtained CQDs have emission in different colors: light purple, yellow and blue. By FT-IR analysis was determined that carbonyl groups from betalainic compounds present in beetroot fruit were responsible of surface passivation and consequently the presence of photoluminescent properties. Raman spectroscopy showed that the obtained CQDs are mainly made up of sp2 graphitic carbon with sp3 carbon defects and oxygen functional groups. The obtained CQDs were used in model Cu2+ and Ag+ solutions with the aim to study relationship of time and temperature carbonization with decrease of intensity emission to stablish their potential use as a sensor of metallic ions. The novelty of this work lies in obtaining CQDs by organic waste, which offers a solution from biomass waste disposal problems, especially in developing countries where these wastes are increasing considerably, and in addition the obtained product can be used as a metal ions sensor.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, in the optoelectronics devices era, high performance semiconducting materials are need, which are synthesized chemically from nanomaterials and quantum dots colloids whose obtaining involves use of toxic reactants with very special lab conditions. However, Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are advanced carbon-based nanomaterials that allows the obtaining of equal or better optoelectronic properties compared with shown by semiconductor materials.
CDQs were discovered from the components of fluorescent nanoparticles during single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 and in 2006, they were properly called as “Carbon Quantum Dost” [1].

Since the discovery of CQDs, many methods of their synthesis have been developed. In general, these methods are clarified in two groups: a) top-down and b) bottom-up method [2]. In the first one, the bulk material is reduced into small size generally by physical ways, and in the second method, the particles are obtained from monomers through chemical reactions. Some of the most common methods for synthesis of CQDs present shortcomings or limitations for an ecological and low-cost synthesis process, for example, high temperature: Arc Discharge, Laser Ablation methods; toxic compounds: Acidic Oxidation, Solvothermal methods; special lab facilities: Microwave pyrolysis, Arc Discharge, Laser Ablation methods, etc. Considering all the above limitations, in this research, we use the combustion/thermal route for synthesis of CQDs, due to the easy procedure, low cost, ecological friendly.

Carbon-based materials and CQDs are commonly obtained from nonrenewable sources such as coal [2], petroleum coke [3,4] and rarely by natural gas [5], however, by the sharp decline of these nonrenewable sources, biomass waste has been potentiated as a new carbon source [6]. The use of biomass for obtaining value-added materials carbon-based can suggest a solution for biomass wastes disposal problems, especially in developing countries where these wastes are increasing considerably.

In this work, we use beetroot fruit bagasse as carbon due to their high content of betalains, which allows surface passivation of CQDs without the need of any post-synthesis acidic process. Coupled with this, in last years, consumption of beetroot juice has been increased due to health-promoting potential, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, anticarcinogenic, hepato-protective and wound healing properties [2], which means that after mechanical extraction of beetroot for juice obtaining, a large amount of bagasse rich in fiber and anthocyanins is generated, where a small part is intended as organic fertilizer and the biggest part goes to landfills causing pollution of air derived from putrefaction. Therefore, we raise the possibility of transforming an organic waste into a carbon source with luminescent properties which it can be applicable to different fields of science.

Beetroot fruit, with taxonomic name of Beta Vulgaris, is a tuber from Amaranthaceae family [7], where the dark red color of flowers, fruits, vegetables, and grains result from the presence of chlorophylls, carotenoids, flavonoids, and water-soluble anthocyanins and betalains [8,9]. Beetroot is commonly referred to as beets in North America and are also recognized as beetroot in British English [10]. In Mexico is known as betabel, while in Argentina, Spain and Peru is called remolacha and in Chile and Peru is identified as betarraga. This tuber contains biologically active substances, as the already mentioned betalains, flavonoids, polyphenols, saponins [11]; diverse minerals as potassium, sodium, phosphorous, calcium, magnesium, copper, iron, zinc, and magnesium [7,8].

The characteristic red–purple coloration of beetroot is due to the presence of high content of betalains, which are water-soluble nitrogen compounds, derived from betalamic acid [12,13]. Betalains are classified in two structural groups: a) betacyanins and b) betaxanthins[14,15]; the first one includes the betanin, isobetanin and neobetanin, which are responsible of red-violet color; while the second structural group, includes to indicaxanthin and vulgaxanthin I and II, which shows a yellow-orange coloration [16,17]. For stability of betalains present in beetroot under anaerobic conditions the optimal pH is 4.0 – 5.0; however, if oxygen is present, the optimal value of pH is 5.5 – 5.8 [18].

In this research, we propose using beetroot bagasse for easy and one-step synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots. This natural source allows to provide the surface passivation of carbon particles which it derives in luminescent properties presence. The obtained Carbon Quantum Dots were used for sensing of Cu and Ag ions from an aqueous model solution. Copper is an essential element required by humans which takes a crucial role in bone development and another important metabolic processes. In nature, copper can be found in different forms: Cu (0) metal, Cu (I) cuprous ion and Cu (ll) copper ion, where Cu (ll) is the most toxic form with more presence in the environment [19]. Copper is considered one of 40 kinds of heavy metals, widely used in engineering, dye, paper, fine chemical, pharmaceutical, petrochemical, textile industries, etc., which inevitably leads to excess concentration in wastewater [20,21]. Silver has many uses in health care, it can be an antibacterial agent in the treatment of burn wounds, scalds, ulcers, and in the prophylaxis of neonatal conjunctivitis, however, it can be deposited in human body as particles causing argyria (blue-gray skin discoloration). Human mortality has been reported during gestation period (64 mg silver/kg body weight), also has been observed that silver causes skin and eye irritation and some cases of allergic, other reported toxicities include hepatic, renal, neurological, and hematological effects. For this reason, in this work, we investigate the interaction effect between Cu (ll), Ag (I) ions and functional groups from Beta Vulgaris fruit which allowed surface passivation of CQDs obtained for first time from the beetroot bagasse by a one-step synthesis carbonization method at atmospheric conditions, with the aim of being implemented as a possible metal ion sensor.

Use of CQDs obtained from waste biomass (beetroot fruit bagasse) reduce operation costs compared with traditional methods as metal–organic framework adsorbent, ultrafiltration, osmosis, electrodialysis, etc [20], besides, they includes sophisticate lab installations.

Bagasse of Beetroot fruit was obtained from a local juice shop. With the aim of avoid mold growth, it was It was dried at atmospheric conditions. Distillated water was used as a dispersant media. An electric muffle was employed to beetroot fruit carbonization.

In a batch carbonization process, 3 g of dried bagasse Beetroot fruit was placed into a melting pot and introduced an atmospheric muffle. Different Carbonized samples were obtained at 100 °C, 200 °C, 300 °C and 400 °C during 1,2, 3 and 4 h. The obtained product was mechanically pulverized until obtain a very fine dark powder. 0.1 g of every sample was mixed with 50 mL of distillated water and stirred vigorously for 30 min. The obtained solutions were filtered with a membrane filter with 0.2 μm of porous diameter.

The described above obtaining process of CQDs, does not involve using of toxic substances or sophisticated lab requirements. In fact, from organic waste, it allows to obtain carbon nanoparticles with excellent optical properties as obtained for other techniques. In Fig. 1 it can be appreciate the schematic procedure of CQDs obtaining.

The obtained CQDs were characterized by Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–vis), Photoluminescence (PL), Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) and High-Resolution Scanning Electronic Microscopy (HR-SEM) techniques.
This technique was used to obtain the maximum absorption peak of samples, using a double beam UV–Vis Perkin Elmer-Lambda 365 spectrometer with a (1.0 cm) quartz cell from 300 to 600 nm.

This technique was employed to obtain the emission wavelength. The photoluminescence signal was dispersed at room temperature by a spex 500 M monochromator with focal length 0.5 m and detected by Hamamatsu GaAs photomultiplier tube (PMT) model R493-02. A He-Cd laser (254 nm) with 50 mW power was used for excitation.

Analysis of QY was carried out by a relative method based on a known procedure [22] using a reference compound (quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 45 %). The QY used equation is the following:
(1)
QY = Q
Y
ST
×
I S
I
ST
×
η S
2
η
ST
2
×
A
ST
A S
Where, S means sample (CQDs) and ST is the standard (quinine sulfate), A is absorbance, I refer to PL intensity and
η
is refractive index.

This technique was used to identify the functional groups that allow the surface passivation on CQDs to get luminescent properties. The obtained samples were placed as powder on an ATR accessory from Perkin Elmer spectrometer “Spectrum Two” brand and analyzed from 4000 to 1000 cm−1.

This technique provided insight on CQDs properties such as double layer attributes with several hydrophilic functional groups present in betalains (hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl) and evaluated CDs ability to adsorb heavy metal ions. This analysis was made in Zetasizer Nano ZSP (Malvern Instruments, United Kingdom) at 25 °C.
Raman spectra were recorded with a confocal Raman microscope(WITec alpha 300R, WITec GmbH, Ulm, Germany).
This technique was used to observe morphological shape and inter lattice spacing. A JEM-ARM200CF, JEOL spectrometer operating at 220 kV was used.

The different obtained samples of CQDs were dispersed in distilled water and exposed to a radiation beam with wavelength of 254 nm with the aim to observe presence of luminescence properties. Fig. 2 shows emission color of samples at different carbonization conditions. Samples “A” were obtained at 100 °C, “B” at 200 °C, “C” at 300 °C and “D” at 400 °C while 1, 2, 3 and 4 indicate time in hours of carbonization time. It is possible to shown that emission is observed in light violet, yellow and blue color, wherewith obtaining of luminescent CQDs is validated.
The different emission color can be attributed to the functional groups by organic compounds from beetroot fruit, which are present over CQDs surface.

With the aim of identify the functional groups that allowed surface passivation of the obtained CQDs, this technique was employed. In Fig. 3 are shown the FT-IR spectra from the different samples. The signal at 3330 cm−1 is attributed to the residual wet in samples, in fact, intensity of this peak decreases while time and temperature of carbonization increase. The signal at 1609 cm−1 is associated with stretching and bending vibrations from N-H bonds of the amino group (–NH2). The signal located at 2933 cm−1 is due to the stretching of the O–H bond from carboxylic acids (–COOH), while the stretching vibrations of the C = O bonds, from the same group, are observed at 1728 cm−1 [14]. The presented peak at 2050 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of the C = C bonds and two peaks at 1120 and 1050 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations from the C-O bonds which is present in ascorbic acid and phenolic substances, from the bioactive substances in beetroot fruit [15]. The vibrations of the N–H bonds of the amino group belong to aromatic compounds, as well as the signals at 2933 and 1728 cm−1 due to O–H and C = O bonds, respectively, of carboxylic acids, which confirms the presence of some structural groups of betalains such as betanin and isobetanin [16]. The signal at 1609 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of the C = C bond present in the aromatic rings, which is confirmed by the weak signals that were recorded in the range of 1600 to 2000 cm−1, which are attributed to the overtones of aromatic compounds. The intense signal that appears at 1400 cm−1 is due to the carboxylate groups of the structural groups of the betalains [17].

Figure 3. D shows an important increase in the signal at 1400 cm−1, corresponding to the carboxylate groups, and that coincides with the increase in the temperature for obtaining the CQDs, which suggests that the carbonyl groups are responsible of surface functionalization of the obtained CQDs.
Uv–vis and PL spectroscopy were employed to identify in precise way the emission color of CQDs.

This technique was used to obtain the absorption wavelength, wherewith it is possible to obtain the minimum energy to get an excited state of CQDs and consequently luminescent properties can be obtained. In Fig. 4 can be shown the absorbance spectra, where first excitonic peak is present in the range of 300 to 315 nm, which is due to the (π − π ∗ ) transition step of the sp2 aromatic compounds (C = C) [23], while the signals at 500 nm of the samples at 100 °C can be attributed to (n − π ∗ ) transitions that mainly include oxygenated compounds, such as the carbonyl groups (C = O). Both functional groups correspond with those present in FT-IR analysis.

Emission wavelength was obtained with this technique. Fig. 5 shows that most of the samples present their emission peak in the range of 425 to 430 nm, therefore, fluorescence is observed mainly in blue color. The narrowest spectra are presented at 100 °C and it can be shown that as the carbonization temperature increases, the amplitude of the spectra also does. Due to excitation energy (254 nm) in photoluminescence analysis is larger than absorption one (300–315 nm), an electron-hole pair is created, whose highest probability of recombination is at the edge of the conduction and valence bands, which in turn, depends on the material core size [24], therefore, for CQDs probably narrower spectra is related with low dispersion of particle size, in this way, CQDs obtained at 100 °C could have a narrower size dispersion than those obtained at higher temperatures. It is important notice that samples obtained at 400 °C presents the higher emission intensity.

In Fig. 6 , the effect of temperature and time of carbonization is observed for samples with higher emission intensity: 100 °C-4 h (A4), 200 °C-1 h (B1), 300 °C-4 h (C4) and 400 °C-2 h (D2). It can be appreciated that there is no exist a lineal relation between temperature or time of carbonization with photoluminescence intensity. However, the obtained sample at 400 °C and 2 h is better in luminescence intensity.

Trying to establish a lineal relation between photoluminescence intensity with time and temperature of carbonization, the emission spectra at: 100 °C-1 h (A1), 200 °C-2 h (B2), 300 °C-3 h (C3) and 400 °C-4 h (D4) were studied. By the shape of photoluminescence spectra, they seem to be formed by different emission bands, therefore, an adjustment with symmetric Gaussian deconvolutions was made with the aim of identify the constituent emission bands. In Fig. 7 is shown the adjustment, where the resulting curve by the four Gaussians adequately simulate the emission spectra. For A1 and B2 samples, the different bands appear at same wavelength: band 1 at 415 nm, band 2 at 446 nm, band 3 at 485 nm and band 4 at 520 nm; however, for sample C3 the band 3 becomes more energetic (446 nm) and for sample D4 is the higher intense. Similarly, is observed that emission bands at 400 °C This could be attributed with the increase in the carbonization degree of CQDs according to the time and temperature, decrease the amount of oxygen and, therefore, surface passivation for the functional groups from beetroot fruit.

According with literature, different bands of emission could be attributed to the first state represents the electronic transitions from the conduction band to the valence band (recombination of carriers) within the core of the nanoparticle, the second and third bands were associated with the trap states created at the surface, especially by protonated oxygen groups.

For this analysis, A1, B2, C3 and D4 samples were excited at 254 nm. Data of QY of quinine in 0.1 M H2SO4 is 54 %. Ther results of QY calculated were 23.7 %, 32.2 %, 34.3 % and 35.1 %, respectively, these values are compared with other research where report obtaining of CQDs by different kind of biomass, therefore, beetroot fruit is a good biomass source for obtaining CQDs with good luminescent properties.

The A1, B2, C3 and D4 samples were analyzed at 25 °C and carried out at pH = 8.5 due to the emission intensity is higher than at acids values [25]. The surface charge of CQDs samples were obtained as –22.7 mV, –22.2 mV, −20.8 mV and −25.9 mV respectively, the relatively high negative zeta potential values are due to dense electron cloud concentrating on the CQDs [26], this indicates an electrostatic stability that allows non aggregation of CQDs, while particles with low zeta potentials tend to coagulate or aggregate over a brief time period. In this analysis is highlighted that at basic pH, a stable negatively charge of CQDs dominate and attract positively charge heavy metals cations.

Fig. 8 shows the Raman spectrum of A1, B2, C3, D4 samples, the D and band G band are located at 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1, respectively. The D band is due to disorder, is ascribed from their vibration in carbon atoms with a dangling bond of disorder carbon, while G band is due to crystallinity degree by its connection with sp2 bonded carbon atoms [27]. The intensity ratio
(
I D
/
I G
)
are 0.45, 0.35, 0.64 and 0.68, respectively; according with these values, CQDs obtained at 2 h and 200 °C of carbonization conditions (B2 sample), has a smaller number of defects.
The Raman data revealed that the CQDs obtained from beetroot fruit are mainly made up of sp2 graphitic carbon with sp3 carbon defects and oxygen functional groups [28].

Sample with higher photoluminescence properties (2 h/400 °C), without any size selection of separation procedure, was analyzed with this technique. Fig. 9 shows the obtained CQDs with uniform size close to 10 nm. These results exhibited the average size of CQDs is in narrow range probably due to the high carbonization temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs applied in sensing of CU2+ and Ag1+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained CQDs were used to study the decrease of emission intensity as a way of sensing metallic ions. In 50 mL of 150 ppm of metallic solution, CQDs dispersed in distillate water (10 mL) were added and shaken vigorously by 90 s. With the aim observe difference in color emission, samples with copper and silver ions were excited with an energetic light beam. Fig. 10 shows the emission color of samples after being mixed with Cu2+, it can be shown which unlike Fig. 1, the emission color emission is blue for all samples y could be attributed to copper ions adsorbed over CQDs surface.

For a deeper study in emission properties, the samples obtained at 100 °C, 200 °C and 300 °C and different carbonization time were analyzed by PL spectroscopy. Fig. 11 shows PL spectra of CQDs, before (solid line) and after (dotted line) being mixed with Cu2+ solution. A considerable decrease in the emission intensity is observed, this could be attributed to number of functional groups on the CQDs surface which interact with copper ion.

In Fig. 12 , it can be appreciated that emission of Ag+ solution with CQDs (dotted line) was drastically reduced compared with Cu2+ solution. It can be attributed to a higher interaction between surface functional groups present in CQDs and silver ion.

Quenching or decrease in the emission of CQDs can be due to energy transfer between CQDs and metal ions. This mechanism is presented when metallic ions accept energy or electrons form excited CQDs when they come into contact with them, which causes the emission intensity to drop [29]. Due to copper and silver have varying quenching efficiencies, the obtained CQDs have higher selectivity for Ag+ ions, which is validated with decrease in emission luminescence.

In Table 1 , it can be appreciating the changes in emission of different samples after being mixed with Cu2+ and Ag+ ions. According with results, exist a significative decrease in intensity due interaction of functional groups over the CQDs surface with copper and silver ions, however, for Ag+1 ions decreases is higher. The percent in emission intensity decreasing is higher at 100 °C of carbonization temperatures, this could be due to degree of carbonization which allows presence functional groups that interact with metallic ions.

By carbonization method, CQDs were obtained at different carbonization times and temperatures by beetroot fruit bagasse. By HR-TEM analysis, size particle was observed less than 10 nm with homogeneous shape and low size dispersion. Trough FT-IR study, carbonyl group from betalamic compounds was identified as a principal one which allowed surface passivation. After adding the obtained CQDs to a model solution of Cu2+ and Ag+ ions, a decrease in intensity was observed, it could be attributed to the adsorption of metallic ions into the surface functional groups of CQDs, therefore, their potential uses a metal ion sensor is established. Potential Z analysis showed that at basic pH, a stable negatively charge of CQDs dominate and attract positively charge heavy metals cations. Raman spectroscopy showed that the obtained CQDs are mainly made up of sp2 graphitic carbon with sp3 carbon defects and oxygen functional groups.
O.G. Rojas-Valencia: Writing – review & editing. D.L. Díaz-Santiago: Conceptualization. J.L. Casas-Espínola: Supervision. C.M. Reza-San Germán: . M. Estrada Flores: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Nanomaterials

J. Clust. Sci.

Seed and Their Application as Effective Photocatalyst in Pollutants Degradation», J. Clust. Sci., vol. 34, n.o 3, pp. 1569-1581, may 2023, doi: 10.1007/s10876-022-02339-x.

Antioxidants

Trends Food Sci. Technol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. C. Stintzing y R. Carle, «Functional properties of anthocyanins and betalains in plants, food, and in human nutrition», Trends Food Sci. Technol., vol. 15, n.o 1, pp. 19-38, ene. 2004, doi: 10.1016/j.tifs.2003.07.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Profile of Phenolic Acids and Flavonoids of Red Beet and Its Fermentation Products. Does Long-Term Consumption of Fermented Beetroot Juice Affect Phenolics Profile in Human Blood Plasma and Urine?
Pol. J. Food Nutr. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Płatosz, T. Sawicki, y W. Wiczkowski, «Profile of Phenolic Acids and Flavonoids of Red Beet and Its Fermentation Products. Does Long-Term Consumption of Fermented Beetroot Juice Affect Phenolics Profile in Human Blood Plasma and Urine?», Pol. J. Food Nutr. Sci., vol. 70, n.o 1, pp. 55-65, feb. 2020, doi: 10.31883/pjfns/116613.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Beetroot as a novel ingredient for its versatile food applications
Crit. Rev. Food Sci. Nutr.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Punia Bangar, A. Singh, V. Chaudhary, N. Sharma, y J. M. Lorenzo, «Beetroot as a novel ingredient for its versatile food applications», Crit. Rev. Food Sci. Nutr., pp. 1-25, mar. 2022, doi: 10.1080/10408398.2022.2055529.
